column2: Statement

token caching / Conditional Access timing causing old tokens to allow access briefly until token refresh enforces the new claims. These mechanisms and their interactions are recorded as the cause in the prior cases cited below
Evidence: sign‑out/token invalidation was part of remediation and helped reveal token‑claim mismatches in the recorded cases
Documented‑step citation:
Evidence: sign‑in log timestamps and client context were decisive in prior investigations
Documented‑step citation:

What the records show was done with these artifacts: compare group objectIDs, show tokens with COMPLIANCE_TEAM vs lacking Compliance_Core, reproduce overwrite by re‑syncing in test, then remediate by group consolidation and full sync
Documented‑step citation:
Evidence reference: investigators used Graph/portal to compare ObjectIDs and found cloud-only guests present in the cloud object but absent in the synchronized object
Documented‑step citation:
Evidence reference: label applied after rename and Preserve option not enabled was explicitly called out as root cause in the sensitivity‑label case
Documented‑step citation:
Evidence reference: sign‑in log inspection was central to proving the claim mismatch and token‑cache timing effect
Documented‑step citation:
Evidence reference: test‑user direct assignment was the immediate validation step used before major directory edits
Documented‑step citation:

After reapplying label/group alignment, invalidate user tokens (full sign‑out / revoke refresh tokens) and validate access. The record shows token invalidation was performed as part of remediation and access validated by Audit Team
Documented‑step citation:

After the full sync, invalidate tokens / force sign‑in for affected users and validate access for external guests. The prior case combined consolidation + full sync + token invalidation to restore access
Documented‑step citation:

Revoke refresh tokens / force full sign‑out for affected clients and have users reauthenticate; if Conditional Access policies are causing intermittent evaluation differences, examine CA policy evaluation in sign‑in logs and adjust or remediate device compliance flows as needed. The token/CA timing diagnosis and the effective mitigation (force reauth) are recorded in TI‑1571046 and related tickets
Documented‑step citation:

The prior incidents with the same symptom set were escalated to identity/M365 engineering (second‑level support). The indexed evidence recommends escalation when group ObjectID reconciliation or Azure AD Connect changes are required; that was the path taken in TI‑1583327 and TI‑1569482
Recommendation (documented): classify this ticket as second_level_support and involve your Identity/Entra team and the group owner and Purview/Compliance owner for coordinated change windows
Documented‑step citation:

The indexed records do NOT contain your tenant’s actual Azure AD ObjectIDs, the SharePoint permission export for your folder, your tenant’s Purview label audit entries, or your Azure AD Connect synchronization logs — I cannot perform the ObjectID comparison or the sync‑log correlation without those tenant artifacts. The records explicitly state investigators needed those artifacts to prove the root cause, but they do not contain your tenant’s copies of them

If you cannot run those queries, authorize the Identity team (time‑boxed, audited) to run the Microsoft Graph / sign‑in log queries. Prior cases show identity engineers (System Engineer 14 / BU Infrastructure – Identity) executed these queries to reach the RCA
Why these are required: prior investigations used these exact artifacts to demonstrate the ObjectID/group claim mismatch and to validate remediation (see citations)

Escalate to second_level_support / Identity (BU Infrastructure – M365 / Identity) for steps that modify groups or run a full Azure AD Connect sync; prior incidents with identical symptoms required engineer intervention and identity‑plane edits [Documented: TI‑1583327; TI‑1569482]
Sensitivity‑Label ↔ ACL case (folder rename, label applied without Preserve custom permissions, token‑claim mismatch, remove/reapply label + token invalidation remediation): TI‑1578891
Azure AD Connect / cloud vs on‑prem group object collision (external guests excluded; consolidation + full sync remediation): TI‑1583327
Token caching / Conditional Access timing case (force full sign‑out / re‑auth used as remediation): TI‑1571046
Group nesting / TEST USER direct‑assignment validation (remediation required editing nesting): TI‑1569482

The indexed records provide a clear diagnostic pattern and proven remediation sequence for your exact symptoms (label applied after rename without Preserve custom permissions; cloud vs on‑prem group collisions; token caching). However, the records do NOT contain your tenant’s live group ObjectIDs, sign‑in logs, or Azure AD Connect logs — you must supply the artifacts listed above (or authorize your identity team to retrieve them) before the precise fix (group consolidation vs label reapply) can be executed safely. The ticket should be routed to second_level_support / Identity with the requested artifacts attached.
